title: True/False/NI: The Comeback of Vinyl Records

tasks.instruction: Przeczytaj ponizszy tekst. Zdecyduj, ktore zdania (1.1.–1.6.) sa zgodne z trescia tekstu (T – True), ktore sa niezgodne (F – False), a ktore zawieraja informacje niepodane w tekscie (NI – No Information). Nastepnie znajdz w tekscie wyrazy lub wyrazenia, ktore odpowiadaja definicjom podanym w punktach 1.7.–1.11. Wymagana jest calkowita poprawnosc ortograficzna odpowiedzi.
tasks.text:
THE COMEBACK OF VINYL RECORDS

Not long ago, most people assumed that vinyl records belonged firmly in the past. By the early 2000s, shiny compact discs had pushed them off the shelves, and a few years later digital downloads and streaming services seemed to finish the job. Yet something surprising has happened. Sales of vinyl have been climbing steadily for more than a decade, and today the black disc is no longer a dusty relic but a genuinely popular product once again.

What explains this unexpected revival? Part of the answer is sound. Many listeners insist that vinyl produces a warmer, richer tone than a digital file, although experts still argue fiercely about whether the difference is real or merely imagined. For others, the appeal has little to do with what they hear. A record is a physical object you can hold, with large artwork, printed lyrics and sometimes a poster tucked inside. Placing the needle on the disc and turning the sleeve over halfway through feels like a small ritual, a deliberate pause in a world that usually delivers music instantly.

Young people, perhaps surprisingly, have driven much of the growth. Teenagers who have never owned a CD player happily buy records, even when they do not own a turntable to play them on. For them, a record is partly a souvenir and partly a way of supporting an artist they love. Independent shops have reopened, and once a year, on a Saturday in April, Record Store Day brings crowds queueing outside their doors for limited editions.

The boom is not without its problems. There are only a handful of factories left that still press vinyl, so orders can take months to fulfil, and the records are far from cheap. Even so, the humble vinyl record, written off so many times, has proved remarkably hard to kill.

items.statement: Compact discs had largely replaced vinyl records in shops by the early 2000s.
items.explanation: TRUE — the text says that 'By the early 2000s, shiny compact discs had pushed them off the shelves'.

items.statement: Experts have now firmly agreed that vinyl genuinely sounds better than digital files.
items.explanation: FALSE — the text states that 'experts still argue fiercely about whether the difference is real or merely imagined', so there is no firm agreement.

items.statement: Some teenagers buy records even though they have no turntable to play them on.
items.explanation: TRUE — the text says 'Teenagers ... happily buy records, even when they do not own a turntable to play them on'.

items.statement: Record Store Day is held on the same date in every country.
items.explanation: NO INFORMATION — the text says it falls 'on a Saturday in April' but never mentions whether the date is the same across different countries.

items.statement: Vinyl records are produced by a large number of factories around the world.
items.explanation: FALSE — the text states there are 'only a handful of factories left that still press vinyl', which is the opposite of a large number.

items.statement: Vinyl records are cheaper to buy than streaming subscriptions.
items.explanation: NO INFORMATION — the text says records 'are far from cheap' but never compares their price with the cost of a streaming subscription.

vocabItems.definition: an object kept to remind you of a place, person or event
an old object that has survived from an earlier time
the printed paper cover that holds a record
a return to popularity or activity after a period of decline
to complete an order by supplying what was requested
